claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start: **How many children are you looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit for my one child. His name is Alex and he's 16 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 16, I need to ask: **Is Alex currently in full-time education or approved training, or has he left education/training?**

This is important because Child Benefit eligibility can continue past age 16 if the child remains in qualifying education or training.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's left school, actually. He's not in any education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex is 16 and has left education and is not in any approved training, I need to gather a bit more information.

**Has Alex recently left education or training (within the past 20 weeks), or did he leave some time ago?**

This is relevant because there may be a limited extension period after leaving education.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He left a little while ago - it's been more than 20 weeks now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Based on what you've told me and the official eligibility rules, I now have enough information to make a determination about your Child Benefit eligibility for Alex.

Let me summarise the situation:

- Alex is 16 years old
- He has left education and is not in any approved training
- It has been more than 20 weeks since he left education

According to the rules, you can normally qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 16, or if they are under 20 and remain in approved education or training. There is also a provision to continue Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. However, since it has been more than 20 weeks since Alex left education, this extension period would no longer apply.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment, I'm sorry to say that **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

**For Alex (age 16):** Child Benefit is normally available for children under 16, or for those aged 16-19 who remain in approved education or training. While there is a provision that allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks after a 16 or 17 year old leaves education (if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services), this extension period has already passed in Alex's case since it's been more than 20 weeks since he left education.

**What could change this:**
If Alex were to return to approved full-time education or approved training before he turns 20, you may become eligible to claim Child Benefit for him again at that point.

If you have any questions about this decision or would like to discuss Alex's options, you're welcome to contact the Child Benefit Office directly for further guidance.

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and has left education/training. Child Benefit normally applies to children under 16, or to those aged 16-19 who remain in approved education or training. While there is a provision allowing Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks after a 16 or 17 year old leaves education (if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or armed services), this period has already passed in Alex's case as it has been more than 20 weeks since he left education. Therefore, Alex no longer meets the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 16 and has been out of education or training for more than 20 weeks. Since Alex is not in approved education or training, and the 20-week extension period has expired, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 16 years old and has left education/training. Child Benefit normally applies to children under 16, or to those aged 16-19 who remain in approved education or training. While there is a provision allowing Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks after a 16 or 17 year old leaves education (if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or armed services), this period has already passed in Alex's case as it has been more than 20 weeks since he left education. Therefore, Alex no longer meets the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 16 and has been out of education or training for more than 20 weeks. Since Alex is not in approved education or training, and the 20-week extension period has expired, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
